claim.hypothesis: …because of the winner’s curse problem underpricing exists in fixed price auctions, but the underpricing may be even aggravated in uniform price auctions due to collusion.
claim.statement: The average market price in the UNIFORM treatment is significantly higher than that in the FIXED treatment. Bidders obtain lower payoffs in the UNIFORM than in the FIXED treatment. The uniform price auction is superior to the fixed price offering in terms of raising revenues.
claim.statement_location: Section: 4 Results; Subsection 4.1: Market prices, seller’s revenue, and buyers’ earnings; p. 212.

data.notes: One group was excluded from the final sample (so originally 29 groups).

method.description: The authors run an experiment using treatments of different auction types and then compared the results using two-tailed Wilcoxon signed-ranks tests.
method.steps: (1) Recruit participants; (2) split the participants into groups of four and then subdivide them into three informed buyers (receive low or high signal) and one uniformed buyer (no signal); (3) the rules are explained to participants and they must pass a test before the experiment begins; (4) in the UNIFORM treatment, subjects are then asked to submit up to six bids; (5) they perform this task 20 times; (6) Subjects are then paid according to their submissions; (7) the results are analyzed using two-tailed Wilcoxon signed-ranks tests.

results.summary: The average prices in the UNIFORM treatment lie above the predetermined market price in the FIXED treatment in all rounds

metadata.original_paper_title: Uniform price auctions and fixed price offerings in IPOs: an experimental comparison.
